trajectory.description: Observation: The training run crashed with RuntimeError: index_add_(): self (Float) and source (Half) must have the same scalar type. This is caused by torch.cuda.amp.autocast casting the MLP outputs to float16 while the manually created aggregation buffer remains float32. Analysis: We can fix this by removing autocast (safe, plenty of VRAM) or casting msg back to float32 before index_add. Since we need a reliable baseline quickly and have 31GB VRAM, removing autocast is the fastest path to correctness. We also need to ensure train.py and predict.py are complete and consistent. Decision: Rewrite all three files with autocast removed, fix nothing else in the architecture, train for 2 epochs, and submit eval to get the first primary metric score and iteration time.

trajectory.description: Observation: First eval succeeded with avg_precision=0.0812 using a simple 5-layer MPNN (300 hidden, no dropout, mean pool only) trained for 2 epochs. The pure-PyTorch pipeline works end-to-end. Analysis: The score is low because the architecture lacks residual connections, uses simplistic message passing (addition instead of concatenation+MLP), has no regularization, and only mean pooling. Standard GNN baselines on ogbg-molpcba typically reach 0.20+ AP with residual connections, concatenated edge features, mean+max pooling, and dropout. Decision: Implement a stronger architecture with (1) residual MPNN layers, (2) concatenation of node+edge features in messages, (3) mean+max global pooling, (4) dropout=0.1, (5) weight decay and cosine LR schedule. Train for 10 epochs to measure improvement and iteration time (~35 min estimated), then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 7 achieved avg_precision=0.1767 with a 5-layer, 300-dim MPNN trained for 10 epochs. Validation AP was still increasing at epoch 10 (0.1885), indicating underfitting rather than overfitting. The model is very small (~3M params) and can be scaled up significantly. Analysis: Key improvements to try now are (1) increase model capacity (hidden_dim 400, 6 layers), (2) replace BatchNorm with LayerNorm which is more stable for graph batches, (3) switch message aggregation from sum to mean to reduce variance, (4) add gradient clipping and AdamW with cosine annealing, (5) train longer (20 epochs) since validation loss was still improving. These changes are low-risk and should yield a sizable boost. Decision: Update model.py and train.py with the above changes, keep predict.py unchanged, train for 20 epochs, and submit eval.

Validation AP peaked at epoch 18 (0.2079) and was still improving until epoch 20, suggesting the model can benefit from more capacity and longer training. Analysis: The model is tiny (~3-5M params) versus the 1B limit. Key improvements with high ROI are (1) adding a virtual node to act as global readout/write, which is known to significantly boost molecular GNNs on OGB, (2) scaling width to 512 and depth to 8, (3) training for 30 epochs with warmup+cosine LR and dropout 0.3 for regularization. Predict.py forward signature is unchanged so it remains compatible. Decision: Update model.py with virtual node and larger architecture, rewrite train.py with 512 batch size, 30 epochs, warmup+cosine schedule, and proper logging. Keep predict.py unchanged. trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 10 completed training a 512-dim 8-layer MPNN with virtual node for 30 epochs. Validation AP peaked at 0.2303 (epoch 27) and ended at 0.2292. Training loss continued to decrease steadily. The run took ~1442s (~24 min). No eval has been submitted for this checkpoint yet. Analysis: I need the test-set avg_precision for this stronger model to confirm improvement over the current best of 0.2017. With 4.4 hours and 20 iterations remaining, I have time for more experiments but must first validate this checkpoint. Decision: Submit eval for checkpoints/best.pt immediately. Keep all files unchanged since the pipeline is working.

Observation: Iteration 10's checkpoint achieved test AP 0.2087 (new best) with a 512-dim 8-layer MPNN, virtual node, and mean+max pooling. Validation AP peaked at 0.2303. Training loss was still decreasing, suggesting capacity and/or regularization limits. Analysis: To push past 0.21 AP, I need a more expressive architecture and better handling of extreme class imbalance. Key improvements: (1) Scale width to 768 to increase capacity; (2) Add Jumping Knowledge (JK) connections that concatenate all layer outputs and project back, capturing multi-scale structural information; (3) Switch aggregation from mean to sum (more expressive, LayerNorm stabilizes); (4) Introduce edge dropout (0.1) as graph augmentation; (5) Replace BCE with FocalLoss (gamma=2, alpha=0.75) to focus on hard positives given 1.4% prevalence; (6) Maintain an EMA of weights (decay=0.999) and validate/save the EMA model, which often generalizes better; (7) Increase dropout to 0.4 and weight decay to 3e-5 to combat the val-test gap; (8) Train for 50 epochs with 5-epoch warmup + cosine LR. Estimated time ~80-90 min. With 4.4 hours left, this is affordable. Decision: Update model.py and train.py with the above, keep predict.py compatible, and start the 50-epoch training run.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 12's run with FocalLoss collapsed: val AP peaked at 0.0275 and training loss flatlined at 0.0087, a massive regression from the 0.2087 test AP in iteration 10. Analysis: FocalLoss (gamma=2, alpha=0.75) is the culprit—it down-weights easy negatives so aggressively that the model learns to predict all negatives, yielding artificially low loss and near-zero AP. The architectural upgrades (JK, wider network, virtual node) are sound but were undermined by the broken loss. Decision: Revert to standard BCE. Keep the larger architecture (768-dim, 8 layers, JK, virtual node, mean+max pool) to push beyond the previous 0.20 baseline. Use batch size 1024 to cut wall-clock time (~30-40 min for 30 epochs). Train with AdamW, cosine LR with warmup, gradient clipping, and save the best val-AP checkpoint.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 13's 768-dim 8-layer MPNN with JK and virtual node achieved test AP 0.2383 (new best) after 30 epochs. Validation AP was 0.2472 and still improving, while training loss was steadily decreasing. The LR in the 30-epoch schedule had already decayed to near-zero by epoch 30, suggesting the model simply needed more epochs with a gentler annealing schedule to continue improving. Analysis: The architecture is solid and well under the 1B param limit (~31M). The highest-ROI, lowest-risk improvement is to train the same architecture for 50 epochs (67% more training) while maintaining a cosine LR schedule that is still meaningful at epoch 30 (~4e-4). Adding an EMA of weights (decay=0.999) and saving both the best validation checkpoint and its EMA version should further improve generalization. At test time, predict.py will ensemble the two available states if present. Decision: Keep model.py architecture unchanged. Update train.py to run 50 epochs with EMA and dict-style checkpointing. Update predict.py to handle the new checkpoint format and optionally ensemble multiple states. Run training now.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 16 eval gave test AP 0.2454 (new best). The checkpoint contains keys 'best_state_dict', 'ema_state_dict', 'best_val_ap'. Current predict.py loads 'ema_state_dict' because it checks for 'state_dict' and 'model_state_dict' first (not present), then falls back to 'ema_state_dict'. The 'best_state_dict' likely corresponds to the model state at best validation AP (0.2573) and might generalize slightly differently/better than the final EMA weights. We have ~38 minutes left — too little for training, but enough for one more eval with an improved inference strategy. Analysis: Ensembling the best regular checkpoint and the EMA checkpoint by averaging predictions at inference time is a zero-cost technique that often improves robustness and can squeeze out a small AP gain. Since both state dicts are available in the same file, we can load each sequentially, accumulate predictions, and average. We should also prefer 'best_state_dict' if we ever need a single model. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to robustly extract all available state dicts, average their predictions, and submit eval. Do not modify model.py or train.py.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 18's ensemble of best+ema state dicts returned test AP 0.2452, slightly below the current best test AP of 0.2454 achieved by a single model in iteration 16. With only ~37 minutes remaining, another full training run is too risky. Analysis: Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) is a zero-training-cost technique that can improve generalization by averaging predictions from multiple stochastic forward passes (dropout + edge_dropout active). Since the model uses LayerNorm (no running statistics), running in train mode during inference is safe and only introduces beneficial stochasticity. The test set is ~44k graphs, so even 16 forward passes at batch size 512 will complete in just a few minutes. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to load only the 'best_state_dict' (the checkpoint corresponding to peak validation AP), run 16 stochastic forward passes with model.train(), average the predictions, and submit eval immediately. model.py and train.py are left unchanged.

Remaining time is ~35 minutes — too short for any new training run. Analysis: The highest-probability zero-cost improvement left is to ensemble TTA predictions from both the best validation checkpoint and the EMA weights saved in the same file. Averaging 16 TTA runs from each state (32 total) should reduce variance further and potentially squeeze out a small gain. Time budget allows this (~4-5 min). Decision: Update predict.py to sequentially load both available state dicts (best + ema), run 16 TTA passes per state, average all predictions, and submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 20 achieved a new best test AP of 0.2495 by ensembling TTA predictions from both best_state_dict and ema_state_dict (16 runs each, 32 total). Remaining time is ~31 minutes — too short for another full training run, but enough for additional low-risk inference optimization. Analysis: TTA worked well (+0.0016 over single-model TTA). Increasing the number of stochastic forward passes per model from 16 to 32 should further reduce prediction variance and may squeeze out additional AP. To keep wall-clock time reasonable, I will also raise the inference batch size from 512 to 1024, which easily fits in 31 GB VRAM. Decision: Update predict.py with batch_size=1024 and tta_runs_per_model=32, then immediately submit eval on the existing checkpoints/best.pt. If this succeeds with time to spare, I will attempt a short fine-tuning run next.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 22 (64 TTA runs per model) produced 0.2522 AP, slightly below the current best of 0.2533 from iteration 21 (32 runs per model). Additional TTA appears to have plateaued. Analysis: A simple, zero-cost inference refinement is to average model logits before applying the sigmoid rather than averaging probabilities. Logit averaging preserves relative ranking margins and often yields better AP for binary ranking tasks. We revert to the proven 32-run-per-model configuration but accumulate logits instead of probabilities. Decision: Update predict.py to accumulate float64 logits across both checkpoint states and all TTA runs, apply a numerically stable sigmoid only at the end, then submit eval on checkpoints/best.pt immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 23 (logit averaging TTA) scored 0.2489 AP, below the current best of 0.2533 from iteration 21 (probability averaging, 32 TTA runs per model x 2 models). Only ~12 minutes remain, so no training is possible. Analysis: Probability averaging works better than logit averaging for this model/task. A novel, zero-cost inference improvement not yet tried is weight-space averaging (model soup) between the best validation checkpoint and its EMA counterpart, followed by TTA. Because the model uses LayerNorm (no running statistics), weight averaging is safe and can produce a model that generalizes better than prediction ensembling. I will create a 50/50 weight soup from the two state dicts, run 64 stochastic forward passes on the soup (equivalent total compute to the best prior eval), average probabilities, and submit eval. This is the last high-ROI experiment possible in the remaining time.
